passages.explanation: Pilate has Jesus flogged — a brutal punishment involving a whip embedded with bone or metal. The soldiers mock him by weaving a crown from thorns, dressing him in a purple robe (the colour of royalty), and repeatedly slapping him while sarcastically hailing him as King of the Jews.

Pilate brings Jesus out to the crowd and states again that he finds no basis for charges. He presents Jesus — beaten, bleeding, wearing the thorn crown and purple robe — and says 'Behold, the man.' The chief priests and officers shout for crucifixion. Pilate tells them to do it themselves since he finds no guilt. They reveal their real charge: Jesus claimed to be the Son of God, which under their law deserves death.

Hearing the claim about the Son of God makes Pilate more afraid. He takes Jesus back inside and asks where he's from. Jesus doesn't answer. Pilate reminds him that he has the power to release or crucify him. Jesus responds that Pilate would have no power at all unless it was given from above — and that the one who handed Jesus over bears greater guilt. Jesus acknowledges Pilate's authority while making clear it's derived, not ultimate.

Pilate tries to release Jesus, but the crowd delivers the threat that forces his hand: if he releases someone who claims to be a king, he's no friend of Caesar. This is a political accusation that could end Pilate's career. Pilate brings Jesus out to the judgment seat and presents him one last time: 'Behold, your King.' They shout for crucifixion. Pilate asks if he should crucify their king. The chief priests respond: 'We have no king but Caesar' — an extraordinary statement from Jewish religious leaders, effectively renouncing their own theological position that God alone is Israel's king. Pilate hands Jesus over.

Jesus carries his own cross to Golgotha — 'The Place of a Skull.' He's crucified between two others. Pilate has a sign posted on the cross in Hebrew, Latin, and Greek: 'Jesus of Nazareth, the King of the Jews.' The chief priests object and want it changed to say Jesus only claimed to be king. Pilate refuses: 'What I have written, I have written.' The sign stands as written, visible to everyone in every major language of the region.

The soldiers divide Jesus' clothes into four parts, one for each soldier. His tunic is seamless, woven in one piece, so rather than tearing it they cast lots for it. John notes this fulfils the Scripture from Psalm 22 about dividing garments and casting lots for clothing.

Standing near the cross are Jesus' mother, her sister, Mary the wife of Clopas, and Mary Magdalene. Jesus sees his mother and the disciple he loved standing together. He tells his mother 'Behold, your son' and tells the disciple 'Behold, your mother.' From that point, the disciple takes her into his home. Even while dying, Jesus provides for his mother's care.

Jesus says 'I am thirsty' — John notes this fulfils Scripture. They lift a sponge soaked in vinegar to his mouth on a hyssop branch. After receiving it, Jesus says 'It is finished,' bows his head, and gives up his spirit. The phrase 'it is finished' isn't defeat — it's completion. The work he came to do is done.

Because it's the day before a special Sabbath, the Jewish leaders ask Pilate to have the crucified men's legs broken to hasten death so the bodies can be removed. The soldiers break the legs of the two men crucified with Jesus, but find Jesus already dead and don't break his. Instead, one soldier pierces his side with a spear and blood and water flow out. John emphasises he personally witnessed this — his testimony is true. He connects these details to two Scriptures: 'Not a bone of him will be broken' and 'They will look on him whom they pierced.'

Joseph of Arimathea, a secret disciple who feared the Jewish authorities, asks Pilate for permission to take Jesus' body. Nicodemus — who first came to Jesus at night in chapter 3 — brings about 75 pounds of burial spices. Together they wrap Jesus' body in linen with the spices according to Jewish burial custom. They place him in a new, unused tomb in a garden near the crucifixion site, chosen because it was close and the Sabbath was about to begin.
